linguisticChallenges.speechLevelConsistency: Korean speech levels (존댓말 vs 반말) must be consistent throughout a passage. Mixing 해요체 endings with 합쇼체 endings in the same screen is immediately noticeable and reads as incoherent. MT systems frequently mix levels within a single response.
linguisticChallenges.sovWordOrder: Korean is strictly SOV (Subject-Object-Verb). Variables and placeholders in translation strings must account for this — English '{name} uploaded {file}' becomes '{name}님이 {file}을(를) 업로드했습니다' with the verb at the end and particles attached to nouns.
linguisticChallenges.agglutinativeParticles: Grammatical particles (은/는, 이/가, 을/를) attach directly to nouns and vary based on whether the preceding syllable ends in a consonant. Placeholder variables create challenges because the correct particle depends on the runtime value — use 이(가) or 을(를) notation to cover both cases.
linguisticChallenges.honorificVocabulary: Korean has entirely different words for some concepts depending on formality — 밥 (bap, rice/meal, casual) vs 식사 (siksa, meal, formal), 이름 (ireum, name) vs 성함 (seongham, name, honorific). Using the wrong register word is more jarring than using the wrong speech level.
linguisticChallenges.spacingRules: Korean has specific word-spacing (띄어쓰기) conventions that differ from English. Particles attach to the preceding noun without a space, but compound words follow complex spacing rules that native speakers themselves often debate.
linguisticChallenges.dualNumberSystems: Korean uses two number systems: native Korean (하나, 둘, 셋) for counting and age, and Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼) for dates, phone numbers, and large quantities. Using the wrong system sounds unnatural.

dialects.classification: Standard Korean (표준어) based on Seoul dialect; North Korean standard (문화어) differs in vocabulary and some pronunciation

encyclopedic.history: Hangul was created in 1443 by King Sejong as a systematic phonetic alphabet. Its logical design (consonant shapes mirror tongue position) makes it one of the most scientifically designed writing systems.
